general_use_data.specific_usage_other_description: Primary use is solo and small-group backcountry travel, including multi-day backpacking trips in Colorado (Indian Peaks Wilderness, Rocky Mountain National Park boundary areas, and San Juan Mountains), occasional off-trail scrambling and mountaineering on non-technical 13ers and 14ers, winter snowshoeing and backcountry ski tours within avalanche-controlled zones, and canoe and kayak trips on flatwater and mild whitewater (Class I–II) rivers such as the Upper Colorado and Green River sections in Utah. The PLB will also be carried on annual extended trips to remote regions, such as week-long sea-kayaking in the San Juan Islands (Washington) and hut-to-hut hiking in British Columbia. I often go out of cell range for 2–5 days at a time, sometimes alone, and I want a purely emergency-only beacon rather than a two-way communicator so that any activation is clearly a serious situation. I typically carry it in a dedicated pouch on my shoulder strap, tethered with a short lanyard, and perform a self-test monthly at home and before any major trip. It is part of a broader safety system that includes leaving a detailed trip plan with my spouse and primary contact, carrying paper maps and a GPS app with offline maps, a small first-aid kit, and extra food, water treatment, and layers for unplanned overnights.
general_use_data.additional_data_line_1: Typical land vehicle: 2019 Subaru Outback (dark blue), Colorado plate QZT-419, used to access trailheads along the Front Range, I-70 corridor, and southwest Colorado. Often parked at lesser-used trailheads such as Hessie, Fourth of July, and access points near Cottonwood Pass and Wolf Creek Pass.
general_use_data.additional_data_line_2: Typical watercraft: 14 ft green Old Town canoe (often on the Upper Colorado and lakes near Granby) and an orange Wilderness Systems Tsunami 145 sea kayak used on coastal trips (e.g., San Juan Islands, WA) and on larger reservoirs. When boating, I wear the PLB on my PFD, secured in a small waterproof pouch but with antenna accessible for deployment.
general_use_data.additional_data_line_3: I usually recreate with one or two partners but also take occasional solo trips of 1–3 nights. My standard practice is to email a detailed trip plan (route, expected campsites, vehicle description, license plate, group member names and ages, and turnaround times) to my spouse and my brother 24 hours before departure, and confirm by text upon return. I have completed a Wilderness First Aid course and an avalanche awareness class, and I treat PLB activation as a last-resort option reserved for life- or limb-threatening emergencies (e.g., serious trauma, suspected spinal injury, uncontrolled bleeding, signs of stroke/heart attack, or being stranded without realistic self-rescue options in hazardous weather).
